headline: DOE Enforcement Officers Cut by 18% Despite Rising Pollution
context: Parliamentary records show DOE's enforcement headcount fell from 892 in 2019 to 732 in 2025, an 18% reduction, while registered pollution sources increased 24% in the same period.

cards.big: DOE lost 160 enforcement officers in 6 years.
cards.sub: In the same period, registered factories increased by 12,400. Each officer now covers 47% more pollution sources.

cards.big: DOE's 2025 enforcement budget is RM42M — down 12% in real terms from 2019.
cards.sub: Environmental crimes generate an estimated RM8.4B in economic damage annually.

cards.big: Prosecution rate for environmental offences fell from 8.2% to 4.1% since 2019.
cards.sub: Fewer officers means fewer inspections, fewer samples, fewer prosecutable cases.

cards.big: Singapore's NEA has 2,800 enforcement officers for a country 1/450th Malaysia's size.
cards.sub: Per-area enforcement density in Malaysia is approximately 0.3% of Singapore's.

cards.big: Cutting enforcement while pollution rises is not a budget decision. It is an environmental policy.
Like reducing the number of traffic police while accident rates climb — the budget line shrinks, but the cost transfers to hospitals and families
cards.sub: Enforcement cuts during rising pollution shift the cost from the DOE budget to public health and environmental damage

cards.big: Environmental law is only as strong as the people who enforce it. Malaysia's pollution problem is not primarily legal — it is an enforcement capacity crisis.
